abstract: This paper investigates how to operationalize the detection of multimodal coordinated online behavior on social media, where users may coordinate simultaneously across multiple action types (retweets, replies, mentions, hashtags, URLs). The authors propose and systematically compare five operationalizations — monomodal, independent layers, union flattening, multiplex community detection, and intersection flattening — on a Twitter dataset from the 2019 UK General Election. They find a fundamental trade-off between the strength of multimodal integration and the inclusiveness of detected coordination, with multiplex community detection emerging as the most balanced approach. The work establishes the first comparative framework for multimodal coordination detection and clarifies when and how integrating modalities provides added analytical value.
key_claims: Coordinated online behavior is inherently multimodal and cannot be fully captured by single-action (monomodal) analyses or by naively flattened multi-layer networks.
Different co-action modalities provide partly complementary and partly redundant information; no single modality dominates as a universal signal of coordination.
There is an intrinsic trade-off between the degree of multimodal integration and the looseness of detection constraints: stronger integration yields fewer but more tightly coordinated communities.
Network flattening (union flattening), the dominant practice in the literature, can discard structurally important nodes and communities, raising concerns about its reliability.

Multiplex community detection (using Generalized Louvain) better preserves monomodal findings while uncovering additional coordinated structures, making it a more robust operationalization of multimodality.
methods: Construction of a multiplex coordination network with five layers (co-retweet, co-reply, co-mention, co-hashtag, co-URL) built from a Twitter dataset of the 2019 UK General Election (Nizzoli et al.).
User selection of the top 5% most active users per modality; edges weighted by TF-IDF-weighted cosine similarity over user action vectors within overlapping 6-hour time windows (5-hour shift).
Two-step filtering: thresholds on the number of common actions (per layer) and on edge weights (median per layer) to retain manageable, meaningful networks.

Five operationalizations implemented: MONO (Louvain per layer), INDI (independent monomodal layers), UNFL with three weighting variants (unweighted, edge-count, sum) flattened then Louvain, MULTI (Generalized Louvain on the multiplex with γ=1, ω=0.1), and INTFL (intersection flattening + Louvain).

Comparative analysis using overlap matrices with harmonic-mean overlap, Hungarian assignment for community matching, threshold-based labeling of communities as lost/common/gained, and node-level lost/common/gained labeling.

Evaluation metrics: actor coverage, edge coverage, Pearson degree correlation, Normalized Mutual Information, structural community metrics (size, density, degree, weight, clustering, conductance, assortativity), and node centrality measures (degree, eigenvector, clustering, PageRank), with Brunner-Munzel tests for distributional comparisons.
findings: Co-retweet and co-mention layers are highly similar (highest NMI ≈ 0.38, fully overlapping communities), while co-reply is the most distinctive layer with little overlap to others.
Co-retweet and co-hashtag share some communities but also diverge, indicating partial complementarity; co-URL shares only one community with co-retweet and exhibits distinct structural properties.

Even when monomodal approaches detect the same communities, their structural characteristics can differ — particularly between co-retweet and co-URL — showing modality affects roles, not just membership.

Union flattening loses more communities relative to monomodal baselines than the multiplex approach does, despite MULTI being conceptually more restrictive; flattening tends to discard highly central nodes.
Intersection flattening (INTFL) is extremely selective, drastically reducing the network (257 nodes, 27 communities) but capturing only the most tightly coordinated behavior.
MULTI retains nearly all communities found by monomodal methods, adds new ones, and preserves the most central/influential nodes (higher degree and eigenvector centrality among common nodes).
The three union-flattening weighting strategies (unweighted, edge-count, sum) yield very similar results, indicating weight aggregation choice is less important than the flattening operation itself.
contributions: Provides the first comparative framework and analysis of different operationalizations for detecting multimodal coordinated online behavior.
Introduces a multiplex community detection approach (using Generalized Louvain) that allows communities to span layers, fully exploiting the multiplex structure rather than collapsing it.
Develops a methodology for comparing community detection results across approaches via overlap matrices, Hungarian matching, and lost/common/gained labeling at both community and node levels.
Empirically characterizes the trade-off between multimodal integration strength and detection inclusiveness (Figure 2), giving practical guidance for choosing methods.
Demonstrates the limitations of widely used network-flattening approaches and offers evidence-based recommendations favoring multiplex-aware methods.
framing: The paper situates itself within the network-science literature on coordinated online behavior detection, building on the conceptual framework of Nizzoli et al. and engaging with both monomodal coordination network studies and emerging multi-layer/multiplex approaches. It positions multimodal coordination detection as an underdeveloped area where existing 'multimodal' work typically collapses layers or treats them independently, and draws on multiplex network analysis (e.g., Generalized Louvain, Bródka's multiplex metrics) to address this gap.
